title: Hotel Mario 2: Escape From Isle Epstein
description:
Three months ago, I was perusing the dumpster behind a restaurant supply store looking for discarded spoons to resell to heroin addicts. When my search proved fruitless, in an attempt to lift my spirits, I decided to visit a nearby Goodwill. It was there I made a discovery that will change the history of Mario games as we know it.

You see, this particular Goodwill tends to have an exceptional variety of rare and lesser-known computer software. I live out in the boonies, and nobody really pays any mind to stuff like this. It just sits on a shelf and rots until a guy like me finds it. And that day, I hit the mother lode. You can imagine my surprise when, out of the corner of my eye, I spotted a yellowed old CD sleeve with the text "Hotel Mario 2" written on it. The writing was panicked, almost a scrawl, as if the writer only had seconds left to live.

I had heard whispers of this game's existence during my years as a decorated content moderator on Nintendo Porn Galaxy. Back then, the word on the street was that Hotel Mario 2: Island Escape was cancelled partway through development when Shigeru Miyamoto voiced his concerns about the game's uncharacteristically-adult subject matter. Apparently this was supposed to be a "soft reboot" for the Mario series, reinventing Mario and Luigi as tradesmen with hearts of gold who aren't afraid to work a little blue. The game was also expected to be more in tune with modern pop culture, including references to current events. Of course, these were all just rumors...until today.

By this point the only employee in the store, a fine young man named Iggy, made it clear he took issue with the trail of dumpster footprints I had left in the store. After a short and straightforward altercation, I was on my way home with my new prized possession in hand. Hotel Mario 2! I couldn't wait to place it into one of the public library's free computers and see what was in store, hidden for all those years.

This video is the raw recording of what I experienced.

The game is clearly unfinished, but there are a lot of interesting details here. Namely, we can see that the basic plot outline seems to have been recycled several years later for "Super Mario Sunshine" for the GameCube. I'm sure there are other details buried in the disc files as well. I am not tech-savvy enough to pull them because I lived in a Mennonite village until I was 26.

I am willing to sell this disc to any potential buyer who understands the cultural significance of this discovery and is willing to pay accordingly. However, due to my present living situation, I am only able to accept payment in spoons.
